coredata.dc:title: TNT sensor based on accumulation layer and effective distance of FRET mechanism with ultra-high sensitivity
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Renbing
Ji, Peng
Wang, Lingyu
Zhang, Hui
Sun, Jinghua
coredata.dc:description: Common methods of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) detection are based on direct detection to the concentration of TNT. If TNT can be accumulated first to increase partial concentration near the sensor and detected by sensing materials then, the detection performance can be significantly improved. As we know, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is widely used as the sensing mechanism in TNT detection, which has an effective range of 10 nm. Based on this, we built an accumulation layer between sensing materials and TNT within effective range of FRET, where exists a large quantity of amino groups. The Amino groups can adsorb TNT and realize accumulation function. The accumulation and sensing layer played different roles in the detection of TNT based on distinct mechanism. Accumulation layer was realized by forming Meisenheimer complex between TNT and amino groups while sensing layer was realized by FRET. Via both two layers and mechanisms, the sensors achieved ultra-high sensitivity to TNT, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.6 nM and a linear range of 1–20 nM. Compared with pure graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the KSV of sensor in this work to TNT increased for about 14 times. The accumulation-detection path provides an effective method for the measurement of TNT with ultra-low concentration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TNT sensor
Accumulation and sensing layer
Effective distance of FRET
Ultra-high sensitivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of TNT on hy-GQD-APTES complex
The discussion and characterization of hy-GQD-APTES
The sensing properties of hy-GQD-APTES to TNT.
The mechanism of hy-GQD-APTES for ultra-low LOD

Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Key Project of Basic Research and Applied Basic Research in Ordinary Universities of Guangdong Province (No. 2018KZDXM067), the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (No. 2017A010101020), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2018A030313388) and research start-up funds of DGUT ((No. KCYCXPT2017004, 186100037025, 196100040027).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagrammatic sketch of the chemical reaction between GQDs and APTES to form hy-GQDs-APTES. The amino groups which formed cavities were marked in pink color.
TEM image of GQDs (a) The SEM (b) and TEM images of hy-GQDs-APTES with different scales of 1 μm (c) and 200 nm (d). The porous structure can be observed clearly in Fig. 2d.

(a) The fluorescence spectra under 365 nm excitation wavelength of hy-GQDs-APTES, GQDs and UV–vis spectra of hy-GQDs-APTES. (b) Fluorescence quenching of hy-GQDs-APTES by TNT from 0 to 20 nM in ethanol solution (Ex = 360 nm). (c) The fluorescence intensity response of hy-GQDs-APTES, GQDs and hy-APTES to TNT with different concentrations. (d) The fluorescence quenching comparison of 6 nitroaromatic explosives at the same concentration of 20 nM.
Sensing mechanism of hy-GQDs-APTES, where part A showed the function of APTES and B of GQDs.
LOD comparision of TNT fluorescence sensor with different sensing materials and hy-GQDs-APTES reported in this work.
The measurement results for 5 samples from 2 different rivers.
The measurement results comparison by hy-GQDs-APTES sensor and HPLC.

head.ce:title: TNT sensor based on accumulation layer and effective distance of FRET mechanism with ultra-high sensitivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering & Intelligentization, Dongguan University of Technology, No. 1 Daxue Road, Dongguan, Guangdong Province 523808, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical Engineering & Intelligentization, Dongguan University of Technology, No. 1 Daxue Road, Dongguan, Guangdong Province 523808, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jinling Institute of Technology, Nanjing, Jiangsu Province 210009, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jinling Institute of Technology, Nanjing, Jiangsu Province 210009, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Physics, School of Science, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Physics, School of Science, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an 710049, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Common methods of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) detection are based on direct detection to the concentration of TNT. If TNT can be accumulated first to increase partial concentration near the sensor and detected by sensing materials then, the detection performance can be significantly improved. As we know, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is widely used as the sensing mechanism in TNT detection, which has an effective range of 10 nm. Based on this, we built an accumulation layer between sensing materials and TNT within effective range of FRET, where exists a large quantity of amino groups. The Amino groups can adsorb TNT and realize accumulation function. The accumulation and sensing layer played different roles in the detection of TNT based on distinct mechanism. Accumulation layer was realized by forming Meisenheimer complex between TNT and amino groups while sensing layer was realized by FRET. Via both two layers and mechanisms, the sensors achieved ultra-high sensitivity to TNT, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.6 nM and a linear range of 1–20 nM. Compared with pure graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the KSV of sensor in this work to TNT increased for about 14 times. The accumulation-detection path provides an effective method for the measurement of TNT with ultra-low concentration.

ce:section.ce:para: As one kind of nitroaromatic explosives, TNT is widely used in military and aerospace infrastructure. However, the residues of TNT in environment can result in many health problems in organisms such as anemia and abnormal liver function [1]. Because of the strong explosiveness and the damage of TNT to environment and human health, the detection of TNT has attracted much attention in recent years and different methods have been developed. The methods used for detecting TNT include surface plasmon resonance, chromatographic separation technology, field-effect transition and chromatography methods [2–5]. These methods, exhibiting high reliability and accuracy, also suffer from some drawbacks such as complex equipment and high cost. Fluorescence methods, developed quickly in recent years, are most famous for explosives sensing because of the excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, low cost and easy operation. Conventional fluorescence methods used organic dyes as the probe to detect TNT for a long time. However, their intrinsic properties such as high toxicity and difficult to be degraded make organic dyes limited in application [6,7].

Quantum dots (QDs) have attracted more and more attention in the past years for their tunable fluorescence emission, high quantum yield and unique electronic properties. As one kind of carbon base quantum dots, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) not only possess fluorescence properties similar to metal QDs, but also show good photostability, low cost and excellent water-solubility. What is more, the biocompatibility of GQDs was also proved in many literatures [8,9]. Besides, the charge action between GQDs and TNT can quench fluorescence effectively. Therefore, GQDs are better choices for TNT detection than metal QDs or organic dyes mentioned above. However, the detection limit of GQDs was usually μM level which may be not enough in special conditions [6]. Conventional methods for TNT detection are based on direct testing to the concentration of TNT. Some strategies are useful in the design of TNT sensor to improve the detection performance rather than choose other sensing materials. For example, Wang et al. reported an aggregation-enhanced FRET phenomenon of conjugated polymer for explosives detection. The response of conjugated polymer to explosives is limited for low fluorescence intensity. However, by aggregation of conjugated polymers, strong fluorescence emission can be obtained which showed enough high response in explosive detection [10]. Zhao et al. reported a framework-enhanced fluorescence of gold nanocluster for the detection of TNT. The fluorescence emission of gold nanocluster was significantly improved by incorporated with ZIF-8 which showed a stronger response to TNT [11]. It can be found the response to TNT is able to improve obviously by structure design or other methods. In this work, an effective accumulation-detection strategy was proposed for enhanced performance in TNT detection.

FRET has been studied widely in chemical sensing and biomedical research [12]. By long distance of dipole–dipole interactions, energy of excited state donors (usually fluorescent materials) was transferred to ground state acceptors nearby with a non-radiative process [13]. Accounted for this special energy transfer mode between donors and acceptors, FRET provides possibilities of highly efficient energy transfer [14]. Generally speaking, the ideal distance between electron donors and acceptors is within 0–10 nm for FRET to occur [15,16]. In past decades, numerous works about TNT sensor based on FRET were reported. Feng et al. prepared conjugated polymer which was encapsulated in mesoporous silica nanoparticles for TNT detection. The mechanism based on FRET was also discussed [17]. Aparna et al. realized trace detection of TNT by synthesizing polyethylene-imine capped copper nanoclusters fluorescent. The FRET mechanism was proved by a decayed curve of fluorescence lifetime [18]. A poly-tryptophan/carbazoles system was designed by Kumar et al. for TNT detection based on FRET, and fluorescence decay lifetime was used to confirm this mechanism [19]. However, as we know, the effective distance of FRET is up to 10 nm. These works mentioned above just used FRET to explain the sensing behaviors but take no advantage of this property of FRET which may be useful in TNT detection. Herein, based on the mechanism of FRET, we report a fluorescence sensor for TNT detecting with ultra-high sensitivity and selectivity via building an accumulation layer between the sensing layer (GQDs) and TNT within the effective range of FRET. The thickness of accumulation layer was controlled under 10 nm to occur FRET meanwhile increase the partial concentration of TNT significantly to improve the sensitivity of sensor. As abundant oxygen-containing functional groups existed on the plane of GQDs, 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) was used to cooperate with epoxy/carboxyl groups of GQDs in the presence of catalysts to form GQDs-APTES complex. And then, GQDs-APTES hydrolyze to form porous structure (denoted as hy-GQDs-APTES) to act as the accumulation layer of the TNT sensor. A large number of amino groups in the accumulation layer can form Meisenheimer complexes with TNT and increase its partial concentration to realize the function of accumulation layer. The fluorescence of GQDs can be quenched effectively based on FRET to realize the function of sensing layer. Via the two layers and corresponding mechanism, hy-GQDs-APTES realized a limit of detection for 0.6 nM, lower than most fluorescent sensors reported previously. Besides, compared with pure GQDs, the KSV to TNT increased for nearly 14 times. In summary, the accumulation-detection strategy provides more effective ways to realize TNT measurement with ultra-low concentration.

3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), N,N’-dicyclohexyl-carbodiimide (DCC) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. TNT and other explosive were obtained from Gansu Yinguang chemical industry group Co., Ltd. Expandable graphite was obtained from Qingdao Jinrilai Graphite Co., Ltd. All reagents were used without further purification.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded on Agilent Technologies Cary 100. Fluorescence measure was carried on a fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon FL3-TCSPC). Fluorescence lifetimes were measured on a Lifetime and Steady-State Fluorescence Spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon FM-4P-TCSPC). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained on IR Prestige-21FT-IR spectrometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was studied by electron spectrometer (PHI QUANIERA II) in the condition of 300 W Al Kα radiation. The morphology of product was recorded on transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Model Tecnai 12). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Quanta 200, FEI) was used to obtain the morphology information of the sample. The N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms were measured by BELSORP-max (BEL, Japan) at 77 K after 5 h degassing process at room temperature. The pore size distribution was calculated according to BJH (Barrett–Joyner–Halenda) method. High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) was measured by ALLIANCE E2695.

GQDs were used as sensing layer and prepared by the previously reported method [20]. Typically, 100 mg of expanded graphite and 10 mL of H2O2 were mixed in 20 mL of DMF and the mixture was transferred into Teflon-sealed autoclave after stirring evenly. GQDs can be obtained after 10 h of solvothermal reaction at 200 °C. After dialysis and evaporation process, GQDs were obtained and dissolved in 20 mL of APTES. With the existence of DCC, and the condition of 70 °C, hydroxyl and epoxy groups on the plane of GQDs reacted with amino groups of APTES. 10 h later, GQDs-APTES was obtained. The solution needs no treatment and would be used directly in the next step. 20 mL of APTES and one magnetic rotator were added in the solution mentioned above which was then stirred for 6 h in air condition. The solution turned turbid gradually and generated a quantity of light-yellow precipitation. Accumulation layer with a porous structure formed in this step. After centrifugation and washing by ethanol, the final product was stored in ethanol solution for fluorescence detecting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of TNT on hy-GQD-APTES complex
ce:section.ce:para: For better solubility, TNT was dissolved in ethanol solution and the fluorescence detection was also carried out in ethanol circumstance. A typical experiment was performed as follows: 5 mL ethanol solution of hy-GQD-APTES complex (2 mg/mL) as well as different concentrations of TNT ethanol solution were mixed and equilibrated for 2 h. And then, fluorescence spectrum of the mixture was carried out with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

5 groups of river samples were collected from two different rivers at 5 locations. After that, the river samples were mixed with different concentrations of TNT standard solution to form real TNT samples. The TNT concentration of river samples were 0.5, 2.5, 6.5, 11.5 and 18.5 nM. 20 mg hy-GQDs-APTES was added to 10 mL of river samples and incubated for 2 h. Fluorescence measurements were conducted and the concentration of TNT was calculated by the Stern-Volmer equation of hy-GQDs-APTES. Besides, the real water sample was collected from the river around Gansu Yinguang chemical industry group Co., Ltd and measured with hy-GQDs-APTES sensor and HPLC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The discussion and characterization of hy-GQD-APTES
ce:section.ce:para: Utilizing the reaction between oxygen-containing functional groups of GQDs and amino groups of APTES, GQDs-APTES complex can be obtained in the presence of DCC. And then, the complex hydrolyzed to generate solid particles (hy-GQDs-APTES) with the existence of water to form the accumulation layer. The sketch map of the formation process of hy-GQDs-APTES was showed in Fig. 1 . Because the amino groups of APTES cannot participate in the hydrolysis process, the formed cross-linking structure was incomplete and filled with cavities to generate a porous structure. BET surface area and total pore volume of hy-GQDs-APTES were measured, which is 227 m2/g and 1.23 cm3/g, respectively. It can be found hy-GQDs-APTES is mesoporous material from N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of hy-GQDs-APTES (Fig. S1a). According to pore diameter distribution (Fig. S1b) calculated by BJH (Barret-Joyner-Halenda), the pores in the product were mainly 30 nm. However, pores size showed a broad distribution. Different pores with a size of 20 and 40 nm are also apparent in Fig. S1b. Amino groups distributed on the inside wall of these cavities and made them show positive charge. When hy-GQDs-APTES particles were mixed with TNT solution, amino groups in pores and TNT can form Meisenheimer complexes by charge-transfer interaction, which exhibits absorption interaction to TNT. Therefore, the partial concentration of TNT increased and the function of accumulation was realized. And then, the absorbed TNT quenched the fluorescence of GQDs through FRET. FRET refers to nonradioactive energy transference between electron donors and acceptors when their distance is smaller than 10 nm. As we know, the bond length of common chemical bond ranges from 0.1 to 0.2 nm [21]. Therefore, the thick of accumulation layer between TNT and GQDs can be controlled to support the occurrence of FRET even they are connected indirectly. By altering reaction time, the thick of accumulation layer can be controlled within the effective range of FRET, which was showed in Fig. 1 and proved by the XPS results. Because of large specific surface area, a quantity of TNT molecules can be absorbed by hy-GQDs-APTES and realize an accumulation function. Even extremely low concentrations of TNT, the accumulation function can also make the fluorescence intensity of GQDs show enough response to low concentration of TNT. As mentioned above, amino groups played a significant role in this accumulation function. In order to prove it, a contrast experiment was conducted between APTES and triethoxyvinylsilane (VETS) in preparation process. A detailed analysis was showed in supporting information (Fig. S2).

The fluorescence property of GQDs was reported in our previous work [20] which emitted blue light under 365 nm UV light. It can be found in Fig. S3a-f, without reacting with GQDs, the hydrolysis product of APTES (hy-APTES) showed no fluorescence under UV light. However, after the reaction between APTES and GQDs, the product color turned yellow obviously. Meanwhile, hy-GQDs-APTES also showed strong blue fluorescence under UV light, which was as same as GQDs. The final product was washed by ethanol for several times. It was for sure that the blue fluorescence is impossible to come from GQDs adsorbed on APTES. Fig. 2 b and 2e also proved that the hy-APTES had no fluorescence effect. Therefore, the light experiment illustrated the successful preparation of hy-GQDs-APTES.

The morphology information of GQDs and hy-GQDs-APTES are showed in Fig. 2. TEM image of GQDs (Fig. 2a) revealed an average size of 35 nm. However, the SEM (Fig. 2b) and TEM (Fig. 2c-d) images of hy-GQDs-APTES showed irregular shapes and an average size of 1 μm. Incomplete hydrolysis leads to strange appearance of the hydrolysates with abundant defects. Therefore, numerous pores can be found in TEM image of hy-GQDs-APTES, which is corresponding with the formation mechanism mentioned above.

The FT-IR spectra of GQDs, hy-APTES and hy-GQDs-APTES were showed in Fig. S4. The C-O-C bond at 1060 cm−1, OH bond at 1394 cm−1 and the CO bond at 1660 cm−1 can be observed in the spectrum of GQDs. The C-O-C and OH bond can couple with the amino groups of APTES under the catalysis of DCC, as showed in Fig. 1. In the spectra of hy-APTES and hy-GQDs-APTES, the double peaks of CH2 and NH2 near 2800 and 3200 cm−1 were marked with circles. Both double peaks of CH2- and NH2 was originated from the antisymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching vibration of CH bond and NH bond. However, the intensity of NH2 in hy-GQDs-APTES was slightly weaker than that in hy-APTES. That was because part of NH2 in hy-GQDs-APTES reacted with oxygen-containing functional groups of GQDs and formed NH-, therefore the intensity of NH2 decreased. Besides, it can be found in the spectrum of GQDs that the curve was quite rough in several zones, such as the wavenumber near 1700 and 3700 cm−1. After forming hy-GQDs-APTES complex, the rough zones were also expressed in the curve of hy-GQDs-APTES. Based on the FT-IR spectrum and lighting experiment of Fig. S3, it can be believed that GQDs and APTES were coupled successfully.

The XPS result for full range of hy-GQDs-APTES was displayed in Fig. S5a. Five predominant peaks can be seen, namely Si 2p peak at 101.3 eV, Si 2s peak at 152.3 eV, C 1s peak at 283.5 eV, N 1s peak at 398.2 eV and O 1s peak at 530.6 eV. Compared with GQDs, there were obvious Si peak and N peak in Fig. S5a, which came from APTES. The atomic percentage of C 1s, O 1s, Si 2p and N 1s was 72.6, 15.9, 5.8 and 5.8%, respectively. The identical atomic rate of Si 2p and N 1s was utterly consistent with the structure of APTES (Si:N = 1:1), which indicated that amino groups did not participate in the hydrolysis reaction. Besides, in pure APTES, the atomic ratio between C and Si should be 9:1. In consideration of hydrolysis process, the value should be lower in hy-GQDs-APTES. However, the XPS results showed a higher atomic ratio value which was because C element of GQDs was detected. As we know, the detection depth of XPS is less than 10 nm [22,23], as same as the effective range of FRET. Therefore, GQDs were detected by XPS indicated that the thick of accumulation layer was situated within the effective range of FRET. This process was described explicitly in Fig. 1. High-resolution XPS spectra of Si, C and N were plotted in Fig. S5b-d. Three predominant peaks were observed in Si element, namely the Si(OR)x peaks at 100.3 eV, Si-O-Si peak at 100.9 eV, and SiO3 peak at 101.5 eV [24]. No hydrolysis occurred to the terminal ethoxy group, therefore the peak of Si(OR)x can be detected easily. Si-O-Si and SiO3 peaks generated in hydrolysis also complied with the mechanism mentioned in Fig. 1. Because liquid APTES cannot carry out XPS measure and no Si elements exist in GQDs, the high-resolution XPS of Si was unable to show the change of element environment. However, it can be reflected in the high-resolution XPS of C. A comparison of C 1s XPS spectra of GQDs and hy-GQDs-APTES was exhibited in Fig. S6. The full width at half maxima (FWHM) of hy-GQDs-APTES was evidently broader than that of GQDs, meaning a more complex element environment, which can be separated into C-Si peak at 293.0 eV, C–C peak at 283.4 eV and C-N peak at 284.1 eV (Fig. S5c). Besides the FWHM, the peak intensity at 286.7 eV of hy-GQDs-APTES was also stronger than that of GQDs, which was attributed to the C-O-Si peak [25]. For N 1s peak, it consisted of two peaks at 397.8 and 398.8 eV (Fig. S5d). The distance between the two peaks was about 1 eV, which may be assigned to N-C bond and CNH bond, respectively [26].

The fluorescence spectra under 365 nm UV light of GQDs, hy-GQDs-APTES and the UV–vis absorption of hy-GQDs-APTES was displayed in Fig. 3 a. The two peaks in fluorescence spectra of hy-GQDs-APTES were quite different from the single peak of GQDs, although the fluorescence of hy-GQDs-APTES had been proved to come from GQDs in the light experiment (Fig. S3). In the strongest fluorescence intensity region of hy-GQDs-APTES from 400 to 450 nm, it can be found that the first peak of fluorescence intensity and the minimum value of UV–vis absorption at this region were exactly at the same wavelength, for 408 nm. Besides, it was also interesting to find the position of the second peak was as same as the fluorescence spectra peak of GQDs, for 430 nm. Double arrows were placed at these two locations. Combined with the discussion above, it can be concluded that the two peaks in fluorescence spectra of hy-GQDs-APTES were induced by self-adsorption of the material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing properties of hy-GQD-APTES to TNT.
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the ability of hy-GQDs-APTES to detect TNT, the fluorescence quenching experiment was conducted on different concentration of TNT in ethanol solution. As shown in Fig. 3b, with the concentrations of TNT increased from 0 to 20 nM, the fluorescence intensity of samples decreased significantly. The response was described by Stern Volmer equation: (I0/I)-1 = KsvCv [27–29], where I0 was the fluorescence intensity of hy-GQDs-APTES without TNT while I was that at different concentration of TNT. Ksv was the Stern Volmer quenching constant and Cv referred to the molar concentration of samples to be measured. However, there were two peaks in the fluorescence spectra of hy-GQDs-APTES. The first peak at 408 nm was chosen as I0 because of low self-absorption and enough fluorescence intensity. In order to show the performance influence of modification, a comparison was made between hy-GQDs-APTES, hy-APTES and GQDs. The Stern-Volmer plots corresponding to the three samples were showed in Fig. 3c. It can be seen that both hy-GQDs-APTES and GQDs exhibited liner response to TNT, but the KSV value of hy-GQDs-APTES (0.0716) to TNT is about 14 times than that of GQDs (0.0051), and hy-APTES showed almost no fluorescence response to TNT. This result indicated that the building of accumulation layer between sensing layer and TNT significantly amplified the fluorescence response to TNT.

In the aim to prove the effect of nitro groups in fluorescence quenching process, fluorescence spectrum of 2,4-dinitrotoluence (DNT) with different concentration were also measured, as shown in Fig. S7, the results state a Ksv value of 0.0323 and a correlation coefficient of 0.949. The Ksv value of hy-GQDs-APTES to TNT was about 2-fold relative to that for DNT. This result revealed that hy-GQDs-APTES exhibited higher fluorescence response to the molecules with more nitro groups. For more proof, fluorescence intensity of hy-GQDs-APTES in the presence of 20 nM of 2-nitrotoluence (NT), nitrobenzene (NB), picric acid (PA) and nitrophenol (NP) was measured, and the responses were listed in Fig. 3d with TNT and DNT. It can be found in Fig. 3d that hy-GQDs-APTES showed the best selectivity to TNT because of the most nitro groups. Although the nitro groups of PA were as same as TNT, which may induce similar adsorption behavior with TNT, the (I0/I-1) value was obviously smaller than TNT. This phenomenon can be explained by the mechanism in forming Meisenheimer complex and different electron donor ability of CH3 and OH (Detailed explanation can be found in supporting information and Fig. S8). The limit of detection (LOD) to TNT was calculated by the equation: CL = kSB/m, where k was a constant and usually 3, SB refers to the standard deviation of 20 blank determinations, and m equal to Ksv in the Stern-Volmer equation which was quenching constant. The LOD of TNT was calculated to be 0.6 nM, lower than most TNT sensor reported previously. (Table 1 ). The influence of pH on fluorescence intensity was also discussed in a range from 7 to 13 absence and presence of TNT (20 nM), as showed in the Fig. S9. It can be found that the fluorescence intensity changed little in the pH range of 7–13, showing high stability in neutral and alkali environments.

The color and fluorescence intensity comparison of hy-GQDs-APTES in different concentrations of TNT was displayed in Fig. S10. It can be seen that with the increasing of TNT concentration, the color of hy-GQDs-APTES turned dark and the adsorption at 530 nm [30] of Meisenheimer complexes made the product turned red finally. The color change of hy-GQDs-APTES demonstrated the formation of Meisenheimer complexes. The blue fluorescence of hy-GQDs-APTES under UV light also decayed obviously at a higher concentration of TNT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of hy-GQD-APTES for ultra-low LOD
ce:section.ce:para: As prepared hy-GQD-APTES sensor exhibited ultra-high sensitivity to TNT, whose LOD was as low as 0.6 nM. Both of the accumulation and sensing layer (GQDs and APTES) in hy-GQDs-APTES played significant roles in this process, which was discussed separately in detail as follows:

The enhanced sensing performance was attributed to the building of the accumulation layer according to the effective range of FRET. As we know, there was one amino group in APTES that participated in no hydrolysis process. The free amino groups generated numerous pores throughout hy-GQDs-APTES. At the surface of these pores, amino groups with positive charge could attract TNT effectively to form Meisenheimer complexes. TNT with 3 nitro groups was easier to be attracted by hy-GQDs-APTES than those with fewer nitro groups such as DNT, NT and NB. That explained why TNT showed the best response in Fig. 3d and PA with 3 nitro groups showed better response than NP with 1 nitro groups. In the preparation process of accumulation layer, a large number of APTES was employed and hydrolyzed to form the framework of hy-GQDs-APTES. That means there were large amounts of amino groups which could attract equivalent amounts of TNT. In the fluorescence detection process, the attraction of amino groups to TNT played a significant role in accumulation layer. This process increased the TNT concentration detected by GQDs extremely. Therefore, although GQDs has a quite low KSV to TNT (Fig. 3c), the sensing layer based on GQDs showed a LOD of 0.6 nM to TNT.

Different from the accumulation function of APTES, the role of GQDs was to detect TNT by fluorescence quenching based on FRET. The FRET process between GQDs and TNT has been proved in several works [6,40,41]. Typically, in FRET process, GQDs were electron donor and TNT was acceptor. Under the circumstances of excitation light, the electrons at excited-state of GQDs returned to ground state and then lead to the excitation of electron at ground state in TNT. Account for the dipolar-dipolar resonance between GQDs and TNT, coupling transitions made the fluorescence intensity of GQDs quench effectively. To further prove FRET mechanism between hy-GQDs-APTES and TNT, the decay curves for hy-GQDs-APTES in ethanol with 5 nM and without TNT were measured. As shown in Fig. S11, the average lifetimes of hy-GQDs-APTES decreased from 1.72 to 1.59 ns after adding 5 nM of TNT. As we know, for dynamic quenching of FRET process, the adding of electron receptor analyte could accelerate the fluorescence decay of electron donors [42,43]. Therefore, the decrease of fluorescence lifetime provides strong evidence that the quenching mechanism between hy-GQDs-APTES and TNT is based on FRET. Although there is a thick accumulation layer in hy-GQDs-APTES and no direct contact between GQDs and TNT, the distance between GQDs and TNT can still support the occurrence of FRET.

Generally speaking, the accumulation and sensing layer in hy-GQDs-APTES played two different roles in detecting TNT to realize ultra-low LOD. Accumulation layer was utilized to increase the partial concentration of TNT by forming Meisenheimer complexes (Fig. 4 a) while GQDs were employed to detect TNT by fluorescence quenching based on FRET (Fig. 4b).

In order to show the application of hy-GQDs-APTES sensor to real sample, different river samples with known concentrations of TNT were measured by hy-GQDs-APTES. The results were displayed in Table 2 . The concentration detected by hy-GQDs-APTES was consistent with the known concentrations, demonstrating that hy-GQDs-APTES can act as a useful TNT sensor in practical detection in low concentration. Besides, the measurement of river sample around chemical industry by hy-GQDs-APTES sensor and HPLC was showed in Table 3 . It can be found the result by hy-GQDs-APTES sensor is only a little lower than that by HPLC, indicating a high accuracy of the sensor reported in this work.

In this work, an effective TNT sensor was developed by building accumulation layer between sensing materials and TNT based on the effective distance of FRET. Integrating the accumulation function of APTES based on forming Meisenheimer complexes and fluorescence detection function of GQDs based on FRET, the hy-GQDs-APTES sensor realized recognition and determination to TNT with ultra-low LOD and excellent selectivity. The LOD to TNT is only 0.6 nM, which is lower than most sensors reported previously. The fluorescence sensor based on hy-GQDs-APTES exhibits several advantages such as low-cost, facile synthesis process, low toxicity, environment friendly and high sensitivity as well as high selectivity to TNT. The combination of accumulation and detection to enhance sensing performance would provide a new train of thought for designing chemo- and biosensors.
Renbing Tian : He proposed the idea for TNT sensor and designed the experiment.
Peng Ji: He conducted the characterization of TNT sensor.
Lingyu Wang: She analyzed the characterization results.
Hui Zhang: He provided the theory guidance of the mechanism in this work.
Jinghua Sun: He is the corresponding author. He evaluated the feasibility of the experiment and revised the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Key Project of Basic Research and Applied Basic Research in Ordinary Universities of Guangdong Province (No. 2018KZDXM067), the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (No. 2017A010101020), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2018A030313388) and research start-up funds of DGUT ((No. KCYCXPT2017004, 186100037025, 196100040027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105706.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
